content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

October 27, 1976

MR. Tom Sade
Sixth District Councilmen
City of San Diego
202 C Street
San Diego, CA 92101

Dear Councilman Sade:

Thank you for your letter of October 12, 1976,
requesting an opinion concerning the applicability of Government Code Section 87207(b)(2) in various contexts. Because
you have indicated that the questions are hypothetical at
this time and because, in any event, they do not pose substantial questions of interpretation under the Act, no formal
opinion will be issued. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(5).
However, I trust the following informal advice will resolve
these matters for you.

As you describe the professional law corporation,
it probably constitutes both an "investment," Government
Code, Section 87034,1/ and a potential source of "income,"
Section 87030, for the elected official. Section 87030(a)
defines "income" to include:

"... income of any nature from any source,
including but not limited to any salary,
wage, advance, payment, dividend, interest,
rent, capital gain, return of capital, gift,
including any gift of food or beverage, loan,
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness, discount in the price of anything of value unless
the discount is available to members of the
public without regard to official status,
rebate, reimbursement for expenses, per diem,
or contribution to an insurance or pension
program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including any community property
interest in income of a spouse. Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of
any income of any business entity or trust
in which the individual or spouse owns,
directly, indirectly or beneficially, a ten
percent interest or greater.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

Tax Union
October 27, 1970
Page Three

You can see that the triggering levels for reporting
differ according to whether the official or his spouse has
the financial interest. However, reporting obligations are
imposed on an official by reason of the interests of his
spouse and these attach even if the filer were to divest
himself of his holdings.

With respect to your fourth question, it should be
observed that the "firm" can be a source of income to the
official even if clients of the firm are not. Salary and
loans, for example, would be reported if and when they exceed $
250 per year. See Section 87207(a). Of course, that minimum
figure is to be interpreted as delimiting the official's
disclosure obligation. If the filer's spouse is the recipient
of the income, $300 per year would be the triggering amount.

I trust the foregoing answers your questions. If,
however, you have further questions please feel free to call
me at (916) 322-6344. Thank you for writing.

Sincerely,

[Signature]
Kenneth W. Cory
Research Fascicle III

KWC:jh

THE CITY OF
SAN DIEGO

October 12, 1976

TOM GADE
COUNCILMAN


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Commissioner:

     Would you please render an opinion on the application
of Section 87207(b)(2), State Government Code, Title 9,
Political Reform Act:

     An elected official is also an attorney. He ceased the
active practice of law on behalf of a professional law
corporation upon taking elected office. His wife is also an
attorney, and the two are the only shareholders in the
professional law corporation. The wife practices law on a
limited basis, and the day to day legal activity of the
professional law corporation is conducted by two associates.
The elected official and his spouse have received no taxable
income from said law corporation since taking elected office.
However, they do draw expenses and have loaned money to the
professional law corporation.

     The law corporation showed no taxable profit during fiscal
1975 and none is anticipated for fiscal 1976.

1.  Must fees to the corporation from clients be reported?
    If so, at what level does this reporting become
    effective for each source?

2.  Would the answer to question one change if the elected
    official divested himself of his shareholdings in
    the professional law corporation?

3.  What level of income from the professional law
    corporation to the spouse would necessitate reporting
    by the elected official?

Fair Political Practices Commission
October 12, 1976
Page Two

4. Would answer to question one change if the corporation
began to show a taxable profit? If so, at what point
would this necessitate reporting?

(a) In the form of salary to the elected official?

(b) In the form of salary to the other shareholder
spouse of the elected official?

(c) In the form of loan repayments to either the
elected official or spouse?

(d) In the form of expenses paid or reimbursed
to the elected official or spouse?

If you wish additional facts or information to render an
opinion, please advise. Thank you in advance for your prompt
attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

TOM GADE
Sixth District Councilman

TG:jk

Because you have indicated that the questions are hypothetical at this time and because, in any event, they do not pose substantial questions of interpretation under the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(g). However, I trust the following informal advice will resolve these matters for you. As you describe the professional law corporation, it probably constitutes both an "investment," Government Code Section 82034,1/ and a potential source of "income," Section 82030, for the elected official. Section 82030(a) defines "income" to include: ... income of any nature from any source, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance, payment, dividend, interest, rent, capital gain, return of capital, gift, including any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness, discount in the price of anything of value unless the discount is available to members of the public without regard to official status, rebate, reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an employer, and including any community property interest in income of a spouse. Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater. 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. You can see that the triggering levels for reporting differ according to whether the official or his spouse has the financial interest. However, reporting obligations are imposed on an official by reason of the interests of his spouse and these attach even if the filer were to divest himself of his holdings. With respect to your fourth question, it should be observed that the "firm" can be a source of income to the official even if clients of the firm are not. Salary and loans, for example, would be reported if and when they exceed $250 per year. See Section 87207(a). Of course, that minimum figure is to be interpreted as delimiting the official's disclosure obligation. If the filer's spouse is the recipient of the income, $500 per year would be the triggering amount. I trust the foregoing answers your questions. If, however, you have further questions please feel free to call me at (916) 322-6444. Thank you for writing. Sincerely, Kenneth W.
